title: About
text:
Hi, I’m Shalini Harwalkar, a Master’s student in Business Analytics at UC San Diego with a background in Computer Science and Engineering.
I’m passionate about using data to solve real-world problems, especially when it involves turning messy datasets into clear, actionable insights. My interests lie in data science, statistical modeling, and machine learning, and I enjoy working on projects that combine technical depth with practical impact.

title: What I Learned About A/B Testing—and Why It’s Trickier Than I Thought
text:
Imagine you’re running a website and trying to improve conversions. You change the call-to-action button from “Sign up now” to “Get started for free”, run an A/B test, and see that version B performs better.
Easy decision, right? At first glance, it feels like it should be, but once you think about randomness in data, it’s not that simple.
The reality is that data is noisy. Even if one version is truly better, the results you observe depend on randomness. This is where frequentist statistics comes in—it helps us separate real effects from random variation.
In this post, I walk through an A/B testing example using simulations to build intuition for key ideas like sampling variability, the law of large numbers, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, and bootstrap uncertainty.

title: What I Learned About A/B Testing—and Why It’s Trickier Than I Thought
text:
Imagine you’re running a website and trying to improve conversions. You change the call-to-action button from “Sign up now” to “Get started for free”, run an A/B test, and see that version B performs better.
Easy decision, right? At first glance, it feels like it should be, but once you think about randomness in data, it’s not that simple.
The reality is that data is noisy. Even if one version is truly better, the results you observe depend on randomness. This is where frequentist statistics comes in—it helps us separate real effects from random variation.
In this post, I walk through an A/B testing example using simulations to build intuition for key ideas like sampling variability, the law of large numbers, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, and bootstrap uncertainty.

title: What I Learned About A/B Testing—and Why It’s Trickier Than I Thought
text:
Simulating an A/B Test
Each user either converts or does not convert, which we can model as a Bernoulli process.


Code
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.stats import norm

plt.style.use('ggplot')

np.random.seed(42)

n_A = 1000
n_B = 1000
p_A = 0.10
p_B = 0.12

A = np.random.binomial(1, p_A, n_A)
B = np.random.binomial(1, p_B, n_B)

conv_A = A.mean()
conv_B = B.mean()
diff = conv_B - conv_A

results = pd.DataFrame({
    "Metric": ["Conversion A", "Conversion B", "Difference (B - A)"],
    "Value": [round(conv_A,4), round(conv_B,4), round(diff,4)]
})

results









Metric
Value




0
Conversion A
0.100


1
Conversion B
0.112


2
Difference (B - A)
0.012







In this simulation, version B shows a higher conversion rate than version A. However, this is only one realization of a random process. If we repeated the experiment, the observed difference could change.

title: What I Learned About A/B Testing—and Why It’s Trickier Than I Thought
text:
Sampling Variability
To see how much results fluctuate, we simulate the experiment many times.









Even though version B is better on average, the observed lift varies across simulations. This made me realize that a single experiment really doesn’t tell the full story.

title: What I Learned About A/B Testing—and Why It’s Trickier Than I Thought
text:
Law of Large Numbers
We can reduce variability by increasing the sample size.









With small samples, estimates fluctuate significantly. As the sample size increases, estimates stabilize and approach the true conversion rate.

title: What I Learned About A/B Testing—and Why It’s Trickier Than I Thought
text:
Confidence Intervals
A point estimate alone is not enough—we need to quantify uncertainty.


Code
se = np.sqrt(conv_A * (1 - conv_A) / n_A + conv_B * (1 - conv_B) / n_B)
lower = diff - 1.96 * se
upper = diff + 1.96 * se

ci_table = pd.DataFrame({
    "Lower Bound": [round(lower,4)],
    "Upper Bound": [round(upper,4)]
})

ci_table









Lower Bound
Upper Bound




0
-0.015
0.039







I found it helpful to think of this interval as a range of plausible values. If we repeated this process many times, about 95% of the intervals constructed this way would contain the true value.

title: What I Learned About A/B Testing—and Why It’s Trickier Than I Thought
text:
Hypothesis Testing and p-values
We formally test whether the observed difference could be due to chance.

Null hypothesis (H0): both versions have the same conversion rate
Alternative hypothesis (HA): the conversion rates differ



Code
p_pool = (A.sum() + B.sum()) / (n_A + n_B)
se_null = np.sqrt(p_pool * (1 - p_pool) * (1/n_A + 1/n_B))
z = diff / se_null

p_value = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(abs(z)))

test_results = pd.DataFrame({
    "Z Statistic": [round(z,4)],
    "P-value": [round(p_value,6)]
})

test_results









Z Statistic
P-value




0
0.8717
0.383397







A small p-value suggests that the observed difference would be pretty unlikely if there were actually no difference—but it still doesn’t prove that B is better.

title: What I Learned About A/B Testing—and Why It’s Trickier Than I Thought
text:
Bootstrap: A More Intuitive Approach
The bootstrap provides a simulation-based way to estimate uncertainty. What I like about this approach is that it relies on the observed data itself rather than assumptions about the underlying distribution.
















Lower Bound
Upper Bound




0
-0.014
0.04

title: What I Learned About A/B Testing—and Why It’s Trickier Than I Thought
text:
Final Thoughts
Version B seems to perform better in this simulation, but the improvement is pretty small and there’s still a lot of uncertainty. In practice, decisions should consider both statistical evidence and business impact. While the p-value suggests statistical significance, it does not capture the practical importance of the result, which is critical in real-world decision-making.
Frequentist methods help us quantify uncertainty and avoid overconfidence in noisy data.

title: Analysis of Student Performance
text: This analysis explores how study habits and attendance influence student performance. Understanding these relationships can help identify key factors that drive academic success.

title: Analysis of Student Performance
text: This analysis explores how study habits and attendance influence student performance. Understanding these relationships can help identify key factors that drive academic success.

title: Analysis of Student Performance
text:
Study Hours vs Performance
The scatter plot below shows the relationship between study hours and final exam scores.


Code
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

np.random.seed(42)

n = 150
study_hours = np.random.randint(1, 10, n)
attendance = np.random.randint(50, 100, n)

score = 5 * study_hours + 0.5 * attendance + np.random.normal(0, 5, n)

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "study_hours": study_hours,
    "attendance": attendance,
    "score": score
})

plt.figure()
plt.scatter(df["study_hours"], df["score"])
plt.xlabel("Study Hours")
plt.ylabel("Final Score")
plt.title("Study Hours vs Performance")
plt.show()

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
This post replicates part of Card and Krueger (1994), which studies whether New Jersey’s 1992 minimum wage increase reduced employment in fast-food restaurants relative to nearby Pennsylvania. The paper is a classic difference-in-differences design: New Jersey is the treated group, Pennsylvania is the comparison group, and employment is observed before and after the policy change. :contentReferenceoaicite:1
This post does three things. First, it loads and cleans the original data. Second, it replicates the main difference-in-differences result using full-time-equivalent employment. Third, it adds a graph of mean employment before and after the policy change. The original data archive contains survey data for 410 restaurants, and the public replication script used here computes FTE as full-time workers plus managers plus half of part-time workers. :contentReferenceoaicite:2
Key Insight: The estimated difference-in-differences effect is +2.75 full-time-equivalent employees, suggesting that New Jersey’s minimum wage increase did not reduce fast-food employment relative to Pennsylvania.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
This post replicates part of Card and Krueger (1994), which studies whether New Jersey’s 1992 minimum wage increase reduced employment in fast-food restaurants relative to nearby Pennsylvania. The paper is a classic difference-in-differences design: New Jersey is the treated group, Pennsylvania is the comparison group, and employment is observed before and after the policy change. :contentReferenceoaicite:1
This post does three things. First, it loads and cleans the original data. Second, it replicates the main difference-in-differences result using full-time-equivalent employment. Third, it adds a graph of mean employment before and after the policy change. The original data archive contains survey data for 410 restaurants, and the public replication script used here computes FTE as full-time workers plus managers plus half of part-time workers. :contentReferenceoaicite:2
Key Insight: The estimated difference-in-differences effect is +2.75 full-time-equivalent employees, suggesting that New Jersey’s minimum wage increase did not reduce fast-food employment relative to Pennsylvania.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Data and Empirical Setup
The data come from surveys of fast-food restaurants in New Jersey (treatment group) and Pennsylvania (control group), collected before and after the minimum wage increase.
The key variables are: - Outcome: Employment (number of employees per restaurant) - Treatment group: New Jersey (=1 if NJ, 0 if PA) - Post period: Indicator for after the minimum wage increase - Interaction term: NJ × Post (captures the treatment effect)
The difference-in-differences model is:
[ Employment_{it} = _0 + _1 NJ_i + _2 Post_t + 3 (NJ_i Post_t) + {it} ]
The coefficient (_3) measures the causal effect of the minimum wage increase.
This model is appropriate because it compares changes over time between a treated and untreated group, helping control for time-invariant differences and common trends.

I replicate the difference-in-differences estimate reported in Table 4 (column i) of the paper.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Card and Krueger (1994) study whether New Jersey’s 1992 minimum wage increase reduced employment in fast-food restaurants relative to nearby Pennsylvania. Their research design is a classic difference-in-differences setup: New Jersey is the treated state, Pennsylvania is the comparison state, and restaurant employment is observed before and after the policy change.
In this post I replicate three parts of the paper. First, I replicate part of Table 2 to compare restaurant characteristics in New Jersey and Pennsylvania. Second, I replicate Table 3, rows 1–3 and columns 1–3, which summarize full-time-equivalent employment before and after the policy change. Third, I replicate Table 4, column (i), which regresses the change in employment on the size of the wage gap created by the new minimum wage. As my additional analysis, I also include a graph of mean employment before and after the policy change.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Card and Krueger (1994) study whether New Jersey’s 1992 minimum wage increase reduced employment in fast-food restaurants relative to nearby Pennsylvania. Their research design is a classic difference-in-differences setup: New Jersey is the treated state, Pennsylvania is the comparison state, and restaurant employment is observed before and after the policy change.
In this post I replicate three parts of the paper. First, I replicate part of Table 2 to compare restaurant characteristics in New Jersey and Pennsylvania. Second, I replicate Table 3, rows 1–3 and columns 1–3, which summarize full-time-equivalent employment before and after the policy change. Third, I replicate Table 4, column (i), which regresses the change in employment on the size of the wage gap created by the new minimum wage. As my additional analysis, I also include a graph of mean employment before and after the policy change.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Data and Empirical Setup
The data come from surveys of fast-food restaurants in New Jersey (treatment group) and Pennsylvania (control group), collected before and after the minimum wage increase.
The key variables are: - Outcome: Employment (number of employees per restaurant) - Treatment group: New Jersey (=1 if NJ, 0 if PA) - Post period: Indicator for after the minimum wage increase - Interaction term: NJ × Post (captures the treatment effect)
The difference-in-differences model is:
[ Employment_{it} = _0 + _1 NJ_i + _2 Post_t + 3 (NJ_i Post_t) + {it} ]
The coefficient (_3) measures the causal effect of the minimum wage increase.
This model is appropriate because it compares changes over time between a treated and untreated group, helping control for time-invariant differences and common trends.

I replicate the difference-in-differences estimate reported in Table 4 (column i) of the paper.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
section: Load Data Using the Local Codebook
text:
Load Data Using the Local Codebook

import pandas as pd
import re

codebook_path = "../../files/njmin/codebook"
data_path = "../../files/njmin/public.dat"

# read codebook text
with open(codebook_path, "r", encoding="utf-8", errors="ignore") as f:
    codebook_text = f.read()

# extract variable names and fixed-width positions
pattern = re.compile(r"^\s*([A-Z0-9_]+)\s+(\d+)\s+(\d+)\s+([0-9.]+)", re.MULTILINE)
matches = pattern.findall(codebook_text)

names = []
colspecs = []

for name, start, end, fmt in matches:
    start_i = int(start) - 1
    end_i = int(end)
    names.append(name.lower())
    colspecs.append((start_i, end_i))

print("Number of variables found in codebook:", len(names))
print("First 15 variables:", names[:15])

# read fixed-width data using parsed column positions
df = pd.read_fwf(data_path, colspecs=colspecs, names=names)

print("\nShape:")
print(df.shape)

print("\nColumns:")
print(df.columns.tolist())

df.head()

Create Employment Measures

# convert needed columns to numeric
cols = ["empft", "nmgrs", "emppt", "empft2", "nmgrs2", "emppt2", "state"]

for col in cols:
    df[col] = pd.to_numeric(df[col], errors="coerce")

# keep rows with needed values
df2 = df.dropna(subset=cols).copy()

# full-time equivalent employment before and after
df2["fte_before"] = df2["empft"] + df2["nmgrs"] + 0.5 * df2["emppt"]
df2["fte_after"] = df2["empft2"] + df2["nmgrs2"] + 0.5 * df2["emppt2"]

# treatment indicator
df2["nj"] = df2["state"]

# employment change
df2["delta_fte"] = df2["fte_after"] - df2["fte_before"]

Replicate the Main Difference-in-Differences Idea
With two periods, a difference-in-differences estimate can be obtained by comparing the change in employment in New Jersey to the change in employment in Pennsylvania. A simple way to do this is to regress the store-level change in employment on the New Jersey indicator. That coefficient is the DiD estimate. This is the same two-period logic used in many replications of the study.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Clean + Create Variables
# convert relevant columns to numeric (handles string issue)
cols = ["empft", "nmgrs", "emppt", "empft2", "nmgrs2", "emppt2", "state"]

for col in cols:
    if col in df.columns:
        df[col] = pd.to_numeric(df[col], errors="coerce")

# drop missing rows for safety
df = df.dropna(subset=[c for c in cols if c in df.columns]).copy()

# full-time equivalent employment
df["fte_before"] = df["empft"] + df["nmgrs"] + 0.5 * df["emppt"]
df["fte_after"]  = df["empft2"] + df["nmgrs2"] + 0.5 * df["emppt2"]

# treatment indicator (1 = NJ, 0 = PA)
df["nj"] = df["state"]

# employment change
df["delta_fte"] = df["fte_after"] - df["fte_before"]

df[["state", "fte_before", "fte_after", "delta_fte"]].head()

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Load the data


Code
# your data loading code here
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

cols = [
    "sheet", "chain", "co_owned", "state", "southj", "centralj", "northj",
    "pa1", "pa2", "shore", "ncalls", "empft", "emppt", "nmgrs", "wage_st",
    "inctime", "firstinc", "bonus", "pctaff", "meals", "open", "hrsopen",
    "psoda", "pfry", "pentree", "nregs", "nregs11", "type2", "status2",
    "date2", "ncalls2", "empft2", "emppt2", "nmgrs2", "wage_st2",
    "inctime2", "firstin2", "special2", "meals2", "open2r", "hrsopen2",
    "psoda2", "pfry2", "pentree2", "nregs2", "nregs112"
]

df = pd.read_csv("../../files/njmin/public.dat", sep=r"\s+", header=None, engine="python")
df.columns = cols
df = df.apply(pd.to_numeric, errors="coerce")

df["emptot"] = df["empft"] + 0.5 * df["emppt"] + df["nmgrs"]
df["emptot2"] = df["empft2"] + 0.5 * df["emppt2"] + df["nmgrs2"]
df["demp"] = df["emptot2"] - df["emptot"]
df["dwage"] = df["wage_st2"] - df["wage_st"]

df["gap"] = np.nan
df.loc[df["state"] == 0, "gap"] = 0
df.loc[(df["state"] == 1) & (df["wage_st"] >= 5.05), "gap"] = 0
mask = (df["state"] == 1) & (df["wage_st"] > 0) & (df["wage_st"] < 5.05)
df.loc[mask, "gap"] = (5.05 - df.loc[mask, "wage_st"]) / df.loc[mask, "wage_st"]

df["pmeal"] = df["psoda"] + df["pfry"] + df["pentree"]
df["pmeal2"] = df["psoda2"] + df["pfry2"] + df["pentree2"]
df["closed"] = (df["status2"] == 3).astype(int)

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Analysis
import pandas as pd import requests import zipfile from io import BytesIO import statsmodels.formula.api as smf import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
url = “https://davidcard.berkeley.edu/data_sets/njmin.zip” r = requests.get(url, timeout=60) r.raise_for_status() z = zipfile.ZipFile(BytesIO(r.content))
df = pd.read_stata(z.open(“njmin3.dta”))
df[“fte_before”] = df[“empft”] + df[“nmgrs”] + 0.5 * df[“emppt”] df[“fte_after”] = df[“empft2”] + df[“nmgrs2”] + 0.5 * df[“emppt2”] df[“nj”] = pd.to_numeric(df[“state”], errors=“coerce”) df[“delta_fte”] = df[“fte_after”] - df[“fte_before”]
df = df.dropna(subset=[“fte_before”, “fte_after”, “nj”, “delta_fte”]).copy() df[“nj”] = df[“nj”].astype(int) df[“state_label”] = df[“nj”].map({0: “PA”, 1: “NJ”})
print(“First 5 rows:”) print(df[[“state_label”, “fte_before”, “fte_after”, “delta_fte”]].head())
group_means = ( df.groupby(“state_label”, as_index=False) .agg( mean_before=(“fte_before”, “mean”), mean_after=(“fte_after”, “mean”), change=(“delta_fte”, “mean”), n=(“delta_fte”, “size”) ) )
print(“means:”) print(group_means)
pa_change = group_means.loc[group_means[“state_label”] == “PA”, “change”].iloc[0] nj_change = group_means.loc[group_means[“state_label”] == “NJ”, “change”].iloc[0] did_estimate = nj_change - pa_change
print(“change in FTE:”, round(pa_change, 3)) print(“NJ change in FTE:”, round(nj_change, 3)) print(“Difference-in-differences estimate:”, round(did_estimate, 3))
model = smf.ols(“delta_fte ~ nj”, data=df).fit() print(“results:”) print(model.summary())
plot_df = ( df.groupby(“state_label”, as_index=False) .agg( before=(“fte_before”, “mean”), after=(“fte_after”, “mean”) ) )
plt.plot([“Before”, “After”], [plot_df.loc[plot_df[“state_label”] == “PA”, “before”].iloc[0], plot_df.loc[plot_df[“state_label”] == “PA”, “after”].iloc[0]], marker=“o”, label=“PA”) plt.plot([“Before”, “After”], [plot_df.loc[plot_df[“state_label”] == “NJ”, “before”].iloc[0], plot_df.loc[plot_df[“state_label”] == “NJ”, “after”].iloc[0]], marker=“o”, label=“NJ”) plt.xlabel(“Period”) plt.ylabel(“Mean Employment (FTE)”) plt.title(“Employment Before and After the NJ Minimum Wage Increase”) plt.legend() plt.tight_layout() plt.show()

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Conclusion
This replication reproduces part of Table 2, Table 3 rows 1–3 and columns 1–3, and Table 4 column (i) from Card and Krueger (1994). The main result is that employment in New Jersey does not fall relative to Pennsylvania after the minimum wage increase. In the simple before-and-after comparison, New Jersey’s employment change is about 2.75 full-time-equivalent workers higher than Pennsylvania’s. In the Table 4 regression, the estimated relationship between exposure to the new minimum wage and employment change is also positive.
Overall, these replications match the paper’s main qualitative conclusion: the 1992 New Jersey minimum wage increase did not reduce fast-food employment relative to the nearby Pennsylvania comparison group.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Additional Analysis: Graph


Code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plot_df = (
    long_df
    .groupby(["state_label", "post"], as_index=False)
    .agg(mean_employment=("employment", "mean"))
)

plot_df["period"] = plot_df["post"].map({0: "Before", 1: "After"})

for state_name in ["PA", "NJ"]:
    sub = plot_df[plot_df["state_label"] == state_name]
    plt.plot(sub["period"], sub["mean_employment"], marker="o", label=state_name)

plt.xlabel("Period")
plt.ylabel("Mean FTE Employment")
plt.title("Mean Employment Before and After the Minimum Wage Increase")
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









The graph gives the same intuition visually. If the New Jersey line does not fall relative to the Pennsylvania line, that supports the paper’s result.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Replication


Show code
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import requests
import zipfile
from io import BytesIO
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# -----------------------------
# Load data from the archive
# -----------------------------
zip_url = "https://davidcard.berkeley.edu/data_sets/njmin.zip"
r = requests.get(zip_url, timeout=60)
r.raise_for_status()
z = zipfile.ZipFile(BytesIO(r.content))

# Read public.dat
df = pd.read_csv(
    z.open("public.dat"),
    sep=r"\s+",
    engine="python",
    header=None
)

# Assign column names
cols = [
    "sheet","chain","co_owned","state","southj","centralj","northj","pa1","pa2",
    "shore","type2","city","chain2","co_owned2","empft","emppt","nmgrs","wage_st",
    "inctime","firstinc","bonus","pctaff","open","hrsopen","psoda","pfry","pentree",
    "nregs","empft2","emppt2","nmgrs2","wage_st2","inctime2","firstinc2","bonus2",
    "pctaff2","open2","hrsopen2","psoda2","pfry2","pentree2","nregs2"
]

df = df.iloc[:, :len(cols)].copy()
df.columns = cols

# Convert all columns to numeric
df = df.apply(pd.to_numeric, errors="coerce")

print("Shape of raw data:", df.shape)
print("\nFirst five rows:")
print(df.head())

# -----------------------------
# Create variables
# -----------------------------
# Full-time-equivalent employment before and after
df["fte"] = df["empft"] + 0.5 * df["emppt"] + df["nmgrs"]
df["fte_after"] = df["empft2"] + 0.5 * df["emppt2"] + df["nmgrs2"]

# Keep the columns needed for analysis
df_clean = df[["state", "fte", "fte_after"]].copy()
df_clean = df_clean.dropna()

# Convert state to integer and label groups
df_clean["state"] = pd.to_numeric(df_clean["state"], errors="coerce")
df_clean = df_clean.dropna(subset=["state"]).copy()
df_clean["state"] = df_clean["state"].astype(int)
df_clean["state_label"] = df_clean["state"].map({0: "PA", 1: "NJ"})

# Employment change
df_clean["delta_fte"] = df_clean["fte_after"] - df_clean["fte"]

print("\nClean sample size:", len(df_clean))
print("\nCleaned data preview:")
print(df_clean.head())

# -----------------------------
# Difference-in-differences summary
# -----------------------------
group_means = (
    df_clean
    .groupby("state_label", as_index=False)
    .agg(
        mean_before=("fte", "mean"),
        mean_after=("fte_after", "mean"),
        change=("delta_fte", "mean"),
        n=("delta_fte", "size")
    )
)

print("\nGroup means:")
print(group_means)

pa_change = group_means.loc[group_means["state_label"] == "PA", "change"].iloc[0]
nj_change = group_means.loc[group_means["state_label"] == "NJ", "change"].iloc[0]
did_estimate = nj_change - pa_change

print("\nPA change in FTE:", round(pa_change, 3))
print("NJ change in FTE:", round(nj_change, 3))
print("Difference-in-differences estimate:", round(did_estimate, 3))

# -----------------------------
# Regression version of DiD
# -----------------------------
before_df = df_clean[["state", "state_label", "fte"]].copy()
before_df["post"] = 0
before_df["employment"] = before_df["fte"]

after_df = df_clean[["state", "state_label", "fte_after"]].copy()
after_df["post"] = 1
after_df["employment"] = after_df["fte_after"]

long_df = pd.concat(
    [
        before_df[["state", "state_label", "post", "employment"]],
        after_df[["state", "state_label", "post", "employment"]],
    ],
    ignore_index=True
)

did_model = smf.ols("employment ~ state + post + state:post", data=long_df).fit()

print("\nRegression results:")
print(did_model.summary())

# -----------------------------
# Additional analysis: graph
# -----------------------------
plot_df = (
    long_df
    .groupby(["state_label", "post"], as_index=False)
    .agg(mean_employment=("employment", "mean"))
)

plot_df["period"] = plot_df["post"].map({0: "Before", 1: "After"})

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
for state_name in ["PA", "NJ"]:
    sub = plot_df[plot_df["state_label"] == state_name]
    plt.plot(sub["period"], sub["mean_employment"], marker="o", label=state_name)

plt.xlabel("Period")
plt.ylabel("Mean FTE Employment")
plt.title("Mean Employment Before and After the Minimum Wage Increase")
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()


Shape of raw data: (410, 42)

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)

table3 = pd.DataFrame({
    "Before": [
        means.loc[0, "emptot"],
        means.loc[1, "emptot"],
        means.loc[1, "emptot"] - means.loc[0, "emptot"]
    ],
    "After": [
        means.loc[0, "emptot2"],
        means.loc[1, "emptot2"],
        means.loc[1, "emptot2"] - means.loc[0, "emptot2"]
    ],
    "Change": [
        means.loc[0, "demp"],
        means.loc[1, "demp"],
        means.loc[1, "demp"] - means.loc[0, "demp"]
    ]
}, index=["PA", "NJ", "NJ - PA"])

The key difference-in-differences estimate is the change in New Jersey minus the change in Pennsylvania. Pennsylvania employment falls by about 2.283 full-time-equivalent workers, while New Jersey employment rises by about 0.467, so the estimated difference-in-differences effect is about 2.750.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Replicate Table 4, column (i)
Table 4, column (i) regresses the change in employment on a New Jersey indicator. Following the paper, I restrict the sample to stores with available employment and starting wage data in both waves.


Code
## Replicate Table 4, column (i)
table4_df = df[
    df["emptot"].notna() &
    df["emptot2"].notna() &
    df["wage_st"].notna() &
    df["wage_st2"].notna()
].copy()

table4_df["nj"] = (table4_df["state"] == 1).astype(int)

model1 = smf.ols("demp ~ nj", data=table4_df).fit()

pd.DataFrame({
    "Coefficient": [model1.params["nj"]],
    "Std. Error": [model1.bse["nj"]]
}, index=["New Jersey dummy"]).round(3)









Coefficient
Std. Error




New Jersey dummy
2.277
1.19









Code
print("N =", int(model1.nobs))
print("R-squared =", round(model1.rsquared, 3))


N = 351
R-squared = 0.01


The coefficient on the New Jersey dummy is positive. This means employment increased more in New Jersey than in Pennsylvania after the minimum wage increase. This matches the paper’s main result: the 1992 minimum wage increase in New Jersey did not reduce fast-food employment relative to the Pennsylvania comparison group.

for state_name in ["PA", "NJ"]:

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Load the Data


Code
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import requests
import zipfile
from io import BytesIO, TextIOWrapper

zip_url = "https://davidcard.berkeley.edu/data_sets/njmin.zip"

r = requests.get(zip_url, timeout=60)
r.raise_for_status()

z = zipfile.ZipFile(BytesIO(r.content))

# read raw data exactly as whitespace-separated text
df = pd.read_csv(
    z.open("public.dat"),
    sep=r"\s+",
    engine="python",
    header=None
).apply(lambda s: pd.to_numeric(s, errors="coerce"))

# read column names from codebook using the same line slices used in a public replication
codebook_lines = [line for line in TextIOWrapper(z.open("codebook"), encoding="cp437")]
codes = codebook_lines[7:11] + codebook_lines[13:19] + codebook_lines[21:38] + codebook_lines[40:59]
cols = [line.strip().split()[0].lower() for line in codes]

df.columns = cols

print("Shape:", df.shape)
print("\nFirst 20 columns:")
print(df.columns.tolist()[:20])

# Full-time-equivalent employment
df["fte"] = df["empft"] + 0.5 * df["emppt"] + df["nmgrs"]
df["fte_after"] = df["empft2"] + 0.5 * df["emppt2"] + df["nmgrs2"]

# Keep the columns needed for the main replication
df_clean = df[["state", "fte", "fte_after"]].copy()
df_clean = df_clean.dropna()

# Make state numeric first, then labels for display
df_clean["state"] = pd.to_numeric(df_clean["state"], errors="coerce")
df_clean = df_clean.dropna(subset=["state"])
df_clean["state"] = df_clean["state"].astype(int)

df_clean["state_label"] = df_clean["state"].map({0: "PA", 1: "NJ"})
df_clean["delta_fte"] = df_clean["fte_after"] - df_clean["fte"]

print("Clean sample size:", len(df_clean))
df_clean.head()

Replicate the Main Difference-in-Differences Result


Code
group_means = (
    df_clean
    .groupby("state_label", as_index=False)
    .agg(
        mean_before=("fte", "mean"),
        mean_after=("fte_after", "mean"),
        change=("delta_fte", "mean"),
        n=("delta_fte", "size")
    )
)

group_means
pa_change = group_means.loc[group_means["state_label"] == "PA", "change"].iloc[0]
nj_change = group_means.loc[group_means["state_label"] == "NJ", "change"].iloc[0]
did_estimate = nj_change - pa_change

print("PA change in FTE:", round(pa_change, 3))
print("NJ change in FTE:", round(nj_change, 3))
print("Difference-in-differences estimate:", round(did_estimate, 3))


PA change in FTE: -2.283
NJ change in FTE: 0.467
Difference-in-differences estimate: 2.75


Employment falls in Pennsylvania but rises slightly in New Jersey over the same period. The positive difference-in-differences estimate implies that employment in New Jersey did not decline relative to the control group after the policy change, which is consistent with Card and Krueger’s conclusion.
The group means show that average FTE employment did not decrease in New Jersey relative to Pennsylvania after the minimum wage increase. Pennsylvania restaurants experienced a larger decline in employment, while New Jersey restaurants were roughly flat to slightly higher. The DiD estimate of about +2.75 means that New Jersey employment increased by approximately 2.75 FTE workers per restaurant relative to the change in Pennsylvania.
Regression Version of the DiD


Code
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

before_df = df_clean[["state", "state_label", "fte"]].copy()
before_df["post"] = 0
before_df["employment"] = before_df["fte"]

after_df = df_clean[["state", "state_label", "fte_after"]].copy()
after_df["post"] = 1
after_df["employment"] = after_df["fte_after"]

long_df = pd.concat([
    before_df[["state", "state_label", "post", "employment"]],
    after_df[["state", "state_label", "post", "employment"]]
], ignore_index=True)

did_model = smf.ols("employment ~ state + post + state:post", data=long_df).fit()
print(did_model.summary())


                            OLS Regression Results

Method:                 Least Squares   F-statistic:                     1.947
Date:                Mon, 27 Apr 2026   Prob (F-statistic):              0.121
Time:                        22:57:13   Log-Likelihood:                -2817.6
No. Observations:                 768   AIC:                             5643.
Df Residuals:                     764   BIC:                             5662.
Df Model:                           3                                         
Covariance Type:            nonrobust

                 coef    std err          t      P>|t|      [0.025      0.975]
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Intercept     23.3800      1.098     21.288      0.000      21.224      25.536
state         -2.9494      1.224     -2.409      0.016      -5.353      -0.546
post          -2.2833      1.553     -1.470      0.142      -5.332       0.766
state:post     2.7500      1.731      1.588      0.113      -0.649       6.149

Prob(Omnibus):                  0.000   Jarque-Bera (JB):              761.734
Skew:                           1.278   Prob(JB):                    3.90e-166
Kurtosis:                       7.155   Cond. No.                         11.3
==============================================================================

Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.


In this regression, the coefficient on state:post is the DiD estimate. The coefficient is approximately +2.75, matching the group-means calculation above, so the direction is consistent with no employment decline in New Jersey relative to Pennsylvania. However, the interaction term is not statistically significant at conventional levels (p ≈ 0.11), which means the estimate is positive but not precise enough to confirm a clear treatment effect.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Additional Analysis: Graph


Code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plot_df = (
    long_df
    .groupby(["state_label", "post"], as_index=False)
    .agg(mean_employment=("employment", "mean"))
)

plot_df["period"] = plot_df["post"].map({0: "Before", 1: "After"})

for state_name in ["PA", "NJ"]:
    sub = plot_df[plot_df["state_label"] == state_name]
    plt.plot(sub["period"], sub["mean_employment"], marker="o", label=state_name)

plt.xlabel("Period")
plt.ylabel("Mean FTE Employment")
plt.title("Mean Employment Before and After the Minimum Wage Increase")
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









The graph reinforces the main finding by showing no relative drop in employment for New Jersey after the minimum wage increase. Instead, the New Jersey trend remains comparable to or slightly stronger than Pennsylvania, which visually supports the difference-in-differences estimate.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Conclusion
This replication uses the original Card and Krueger data archive, constructs full-time-equivalent employment as empft + 0.5*emppt + nmgrs, and estimates the treatment effect using a simple difference-in-differences design. That setup follows the same core logic used in recent public replications of the paper.
The main quantity of interest is the relative change in employment in New Jersey compared with Pennsylvania. The group means, the DiD estimate, and the regression coefficient on the interaction term all speak to the same question: whether the minimum wage increase reduced employment. Based on Card and Krueger’s original argument, a non-negative estimate would support their conclusion that employment did not fall in New Jersey relative to Pennsylvania.
